name: Sports Psychology
description: An applied course exploring the psychological principles that influence athletic performance, including motivation, goal setting, mental imagery, team dynamics, and achieving peak performance states.

modules.title: Introduction to Sports Psychology
modules.objectives: Define sports psychology, identify its major research areas, and explain how mental skills training improves athletic performance. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: APA — Division 47: Exercise and Sport Psychology
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Cognition and psychology lesson videos for this topic: Introduction to Sports Psychology.
modules.practiceNote: Read the APA overview of sports psychology and list three mental skills you want to develop
modules.assignment: Write a 1-page reflection on how mental factors have helped or hindered your performance in a sport or physical challenge, and what sports psychology skill you most want to improve. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Introduction to Sports Psychology module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Motivation in Sport
modules.objectives: Distinguish intrinsic from extrinsic motivation, apply Self-Determination Theory to athletic contexts, and identify strategies for building and sustaining motivation.
modules.readingNote: APA — Motivation in Sport — for background reading on Motivation in Sport before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Cognition and psychology lesson videos for this topic: Motivation in Sport.
modules.practiceNote: Reflect on your current motivations for sport or exercise and classify each as intrinsic or extrinsic
modules.assignment: Describe your primary motivations for sport participation. Design a personal motivation strategy using Self-Determination Theory principles (autonomy, competence, relatedness). Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Motivation in Sport module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Goal Setting
modules.objectives: Distinguish outcome, performance, and process goals; apply the SMART framework to sport goals; understand the role of goal tracking in athletic success. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: ACE Fitness — SMART Goal Setting for Athletes
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Outcome Process and Performance Goals
modules.practiceNote: Write one outcome, one performance, and one process goal for your sport or fitness activity
modules.assignment: Create a goal-setting plan for a sport or physical activity with one outcome goal, two performance goals, and three process goals using the SMART format with a tracking schedule. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Goal Setting module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Arousal, Anxiety & Stress
modules.objectives: Explain the Inverted-U Hypothesis and its application to sport performance, differentiate cognitive and somatic anxiety, and identify pre-competition anxiety management strategies.
modules.readingNote: APA — Managing Competition Anxiety and Stress
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Arousal and Athletic Performance — Inverted-U Theory
modules.practiceNote: Practice a 5-minute pre-performance breathing routine to manage arousal
modules.assignment: Analyze the inverted-U hypothesis using a personal sport example. Write a pre-competition routine that helps you reach your optimal arousal zone, including physical and mental techniques. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Arousal, Anxiety & Stress module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Attention & Concentration
modules.objectives: Understand attentional styles (broad/narrow, internal/external), identify concentration disruptions in sport, and practice attention control techniques. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: APA — Attention and Concentration in Sport
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Cognition and psychology lesson videos for this topic: Attention & Concentration.
modules.practiceNote: Practice a 5-minute 'cue word' attention control exercise during a physical activity
modules.assignment: Identify the attentional style (broad/narrow, internal/external) required at different moments in your sport. Design a 3-strategy attention control plan for a critical performance situation. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Attention & Concentration module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Mental Imagery & Visualization
modules.objectives: Define mental imagery in sport, explain PETTLEP imagery model components, and practice visualization scripts for performance enhancement. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: ACE Fitness — Mental Imagery for Athletic Performance
modules.videoNote: University of Minnesota Libraries — Media and Culture open lesson resource: Mental Imagery & Visualization.
modules.practiceNote: Complete a 10-minute guided visualization for a sport skill you want to improve
modules.assignment: Write a detailed 5-minute imagery script for a key moment in your sport, incorporating all five senses and positive emotional content. Practice it daily for one week and record your experience. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Mental Imagery & Visualization module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Self-Confidence & Self-Efficacy
modules.objectives: Differentiate self-confidence and self-efficacy, explain Bandura's four sources of self-efficacy, and develop strategies to build athletic confidence. Students will interpret sources or examples, explain cause-and-effect relationships, and support claims with evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: APA — Self-Confidence and Self-Efficacy in Sport
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Cognition and psychology lesson videos for this topic: Self-Confidence & Self-Efficacy.
modules.practiceNote: Use Bandura's four sources of self-efficacy to identify one strategy for increasing your athletic confidence
modules.assignment: Write a 1-page confidence-building plan for a sport or performance situation, applying at least three of Bandura's sources of self-efficacy with specific action steps. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Self-Confidence & Self-Efficacy module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Team Cohesion & Leadership
modules.objectives: Define team cohesion and its two types (task and social), explain the leadership styles effective in sport settings, and identify strategies for building team unity.
modules.readingNote: APA — Team Dynamics and Leadership in Sport
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Leadership Styles in Sport Psychology
modules.practiceNote: Complete a team cohesion self-assessment for a team you are currently on or have been part of
modules.assignment: Analyze the cohesion and leadership of a team you have participated in. Identify the leadership style used by the coach and recommend two strategies to improve team cohesion. Submit your work as a labeled artifact that includes: (1) at least three specific examples from the lesson, (2) a short explanation of your reasoning for each major choice, (3) one correction or revision based on self-check, and (4) a 100-word reflection connecting the skill to a real academic, career, or life situation. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Team Cohesion & Leadership module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

quizQuestions.question: Sports psychology primarily focuses on:
options: The mental and psychological factors that influence athletic performance
quizQuestions.answer: The mental and psychological factors that influence athletic performance
quizQuestions.explanation: Sports psychology studies how psychological factors — motivation, confidence, anxiety, focus — affect sport performance and well-being.

quizQuestions.question: Which is an example of a mental skill used in sports psychology?
quizQuestions.answer: Visualization and mental imagery
quizQuestions.explanation: Visualization (mental imagery) is a core mental skill in sports psychology, used to mentally rehearse performance before competition.

quizQuestions.question: Sports psychology primarily focuses on what?
options: Mental and psychological factors that influence athletic performance and participation
Only muscle size and body composition
quizQuestions.answer: Mental and psychological factors that influence athletic performance and participation
quizQuestions.explanation: Sports psychology studies how thoughts, emotions, motivation, and behavior relate to sport.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which athlete characteristic is most improved by sports psychology training?
quizQuestions.answer: Sports psychology training most directly improves mental skills such as confidence, focus, motivation, emotional regulation, resilience, and performance under pressure.
quizQuestions.explanation: The course focus is not physical strength or technique alone; it is the mental side of preparation and competition.

quizQuestions.question: Intrinsic motivation in sport refers to participating:
options: For the inherent enjoyment, challenge, and personal satisfaction of the activity
quizQuestions.answer: For the inherent enjoyment, challenge, and personal satisfaction of the activity
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation comes from within — the joy of the activity itself, personal challenge, and the satisfaction of improvement.

quizQuestions.question: Self-Determination Theory identifies which three basic psychological needs?
quizQuestions.answer: Autonomy, competence, and relatedness
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-Determination Theory (SDT) identifies autonomy, competence, and relatedness as the three fundamental psychological needs that fuel intrinsic motivation.

quizQuestions.question: Over-reliance on external rewards in sport can lead to:
options: The undermining effect — decreased intrinsic motivation
quizQuestions.answer: The undermining effect — decreased intrinsic motivation
quizQuestions.explanation: When external rewards (overjustification effect) become the primary reason for participation, intrinsic motivation can decrease.

quizQuestions.question: Intrinsic motivation in sport means participating for what reason?
options: Inherent enjoyment, challenge, or personal satisfaction
quizQuestions.answer: Inherent enjoyment, challenge, or personal satisfaction
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation comes from the activity itself. It connects the correct answer to Motivation in Sport and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of goal focuses on the end result (winning or placing)?
quizQuestions.answer: Outcome goal
quizQuestions.explanation: Outcome goals focus on end results like winning a competition or ranking, which are partially outside the athlete's control.

quizQuestions.question: In Goal Setting, A process goal focuses on:
options: The specific behaviors and actions within performance (e.g., 'keep hips forward')
quizQuestions.answer: The specific behaviors and actions within performance (e.g., 'keep hips forward')
quizQuestions.explanation: Process goals focus on technical execution — specific actions and behaviors the athlete performs during competition.

quizQuestions.question: Research suggests the most effective goal combination for performance improvement is:
options: A combination of outcome, performance, and process goals
quizQuestions.answer: A combination of outcome, performance, and process goals
quizQuestions.explanation: Using all three goal types together provides direction (outcome), benchmarks (performance), and actionable focus (process) for optimal athletic development.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: A performance goal example for a swimmer would be...
quizQuestions.answer: A performance goal for a swimmer would be improving a personal time, stroke efficiency, split consistency, or turn technique, regardless of whether they win the race.
quizQuestions.explanation: Performance goals focus on controllable personal improvement, unlike outcome goals that depend on beating others.

quizQuestions.question: The Inverted-U Hypothesis describes the relationship between arousal and performance as:
options: Performance improves linearly with arousal indefinitely
Performance is best at an optimal arousal level — too little or too much hurts performance
Arousal has no effect on performance
quizQuestions.answer: Performance is best at an optimal arousal level — too little or too much hurts performance
quizQuestions.explanation: The Inverted-U (Yerkes-Dodson) relationship shows performance peaks at moderate arousal; too little causes under-arousal, too much causes anxiety and performance decline.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive anxiety in sport refers to:
options: Mental worry and negative thoughts about performance
quizQuestions.answer: Mental worry and negative thoughts about performance
quizQuestions.explanation: Cognitive anxiety involves negative thoughts, worry, and fear of failure — the mental component of pre-competition anxiety.

quizQuestions.question: In Arousal, Anxiety & Stress, Somatic anxiety refers to:
options: Physical symptoms of anxiety such as increased heart rate, sweating, and muscle tension
quizQuestions.answer: Physical symptoms of anxiety such as increased heart rate, sweating, and muscle tension
quizQuestions.explanation: Somatic anxiety involves the physical symptoms of nervous arousal — elevated heart rate, sweating, nausea, and muscle tension.

quizQuestions.question: Diaphragmatic (belly) breathing is used in sport to:
options: Reduce anxiety and bring arousal to an optimal level
quizQuestions.answer: Reduce anxiety and bring arousal to an optimal level
quizQuestions.explanation: Slow, deep belly breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system, reducing physiological and cognitive arousal before or during competition.

quizQuestions.question: Which attentional focus is described as 'broad-external'?
options: Focusing on analyzing a game plan
Scanning the field to read the environment and other players
Attending to one specific technical element
quizQuestions.answer: Scanning the field to read the environment and other players
quizQuestions.explanation: Broad-external attention involves scanning the environment broadly — reading the field, tracking multiple players, and assessing the situation.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of attention is most useful for a basketball point guard during a game?
options: Broad-external to read the defense and teammates
Narrow-external to follow only the ball
quizQuestions.answer: Broad-external to read the defense and teammates
quizQuestions.explanation: A point guard must scan broadly (broad-external) to read the defense, find open teammates, and make real-time decisions.

quizQuestions.question: Which example best describes a broad-external attentional focus?
options: Scanning the field to read the environment and other players
Watching one small target and nothing else
Reviewing private self-talk after the game
quizQuestions.answer: Scanning the field to read the environment and other players
quizQuestions.explanation: Broad-external attention is outward-facing and takes in many environmental cues.

quizQuestions.question: Which attentional focus is most useful for a basketball point guard reading the defense during live play?
quizQuestions.answer: Broad-external attention
quizQuestions.explanation: Point guards often need broad-external attention to scan teammates, defenders, and space.

quizQuestions.question: Mental imagery in sport is most effective when it includes:
options: Only visual elements of the skill
Only negative outcomes to prepare for failure
quizQuestions.answer: Multiple senses (visual, kinesthetic, auditory, emotional)
quizQuestions.explanation: Multi-sensory imagery incorporating sight, movement feel, sound, and emotions is significantly more effective than visual-only imagery.

quizQuestions.question: Internal imagery perspective means:
options: Watching yourself perform from an outside observer's view
Imagining the performance from your own viewpoint — as if inside your body
quizQuestions.answer: Imagining the performance from your own viewpoint — as if inside your body
quizQuestions.explanation: Internal imagery places the athlete 'inside' their body, experiencing the kinesthetic feel of the movement during visualization.

quizQuestions.question: Research shows mental imagery works partly because:
options: It activates similar neural pathways used in actual physical performance
It only improves confidence without affecting skill
It replaces the need for physical practice
quizQuestions.answer: It activates similar neural pathways used in actual physical performance
quizQuestions.explanation: Neurological research shows that mental imagery activates many of the same brain regions and neural pathways as actual physical movement.

quizQuestions.question: Mental imagery in sport is most effective when it includes what?
options: Multiple senses, including visual, kinesthetic, auditory, and emotional detail
quizQuestions.answer: Multiple senses, including visual, kinesthetic, auditory, and emotional detail
quizQuestions.explanation: Vivid multisensory imagery makes mental rehearsal more realistic. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Mental Imagery & Visualization.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: When is mental imagery most beneficial for athletes?
quizQuestions.answer: Mental imagery is most beneficial when athletes use it regularly before practice or competition to rehearse skills, strategies, routines, and responses to pressure.
quizQuestions.explanation: Imagery works best as structured mental practice, not as a last-minute substitute for physical training.

quizQuestions.question: Self-efficacy, as defined by Bandura, refers to:
options: Belief in one's ability to perform a specific task successfully
Feeling good about your sport career in general
Comparing yourself positively to other athletes
quizQuestions.answer: Belief in one's ability to perform a specific task successfully
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-efficacy is situation-specific confidence — the belief that you can execute a specific behavior or skill successfully.

quizQuestions.question: Bandura's strongest source of self-efficacy is:
quizQuestions.answer: Mastery experiences (past successes)
quizQuestions.explanation: Mastery experiences — personal past successes — are Bandura's most powerful source of self-efficacy because they provide direct evidence of capability.

quizQuestions.question: Self-efficacy, as defined by Bandura, refers to what?
options: Belief in one's ability to perform a specific task successfully
quizQuestions.answer: Belief in one's ability to perform a specific task successfully
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-efficacy is task-specific confidence in capability. Students need to recognize why Belief in one's ability to perform a specific task successfully matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: Athletes with high self-efficacy typically.
quizQuestions.answer: Athletes with high self-efficacy typically believe they can meet the demands of a task, persist through setbacks, and approach challenges with confidence.
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-efficacy is task-specific belief in one's capability, so it affects effort, persistence, and response to difficulty.

quizQuestions.question: Task cohesion in a sport team refers to:
options: Team members enjoying each other socially
The degree to which team members work together to achieve team goals
Physical fitness level of the team
quizQuestions.answer: The degree to which team members work together to achieve team goals
quizQuestions.explanation: Task cohesion reflects how well team members cooperate and coordinate to achieve shared performance objectives.

quizQuestions.question: Which leadership style involves the leader making all decisions without athlete input?
quizQuestions.answer: Autocratic
quizQuestions.explanation: Autocratic leadership involves the coach making all decisions without seeking or considering input from athletes.

quizQuestions.question: Transformational leadership in sport is characterized by:
options: Focusing on reward and punishment only
Inspiring athletes through vision, individual consideration, and motivating beyond self-interest
quizQuestions.answer: Inspiring athletes through vision, individual consideration, and motivating beyond self-interest
quizQuestions.explanation: Transformational coaches inspire athletes with a shared vision, individualize support, and motivate intrinsic values beyond external rewards.

quizQuestions.question: Task cohesion in a sport team refers to what?
options: Team members working together toward shared performance goals
One athlete making every decision alone
quizQuestions.answer: Team members working together toward shared performance goals
quizQuestions.explanation: Task cohesion is about coordinated effort toward team objectives. Students need to recognize why Team members working together toward shared performance goals matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: Sports psychology primarily studies which aspect of athletic performance?
options: Psychological and mental factors affecting sport performance
questions.answer: Psychological and mental factors affecting sport performance
questions.explanation: Sports psychology focuses on mental and psychological factors — motivation, anxiety, confidence, focus — that affect athletic performance.

questions.question: Intrinsic motivation in sport comes from:
options: External rewards like trophies or money
Internal enjoyment, challenge, and personal satisfaction
questions.answer: Internal enjoyment, challenge, and personal satisfaction
questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation is driven by personal enjoyment, curiosity, and the inherent satisfaction of the activity itself.

questions.question: Which three needs does Self-Determination Theory connect to intrinsic motivation?
questions.answer: Autonomy, competence, and relatedness
questions.explanation: SDT identifies autonomy, competence, and relatedness as the three psychological needs that fuel intrinsic motivation.

questions.question: Which type of goal focuses on controllable behaviors during performance?
questions.answer: Process goals
questions.explanation: Process goals emphasize actions the athlete can control. It connects the correct answer to Sports Psychology midterm concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: The Inverted-U Hypothesis suggests performance is best when arousal is:
questions.answer: At an optimal moderate level
questions.explanation: The Inverted-U model predicts peak performance at an optimal moderate arousal level; too little or too much impairs performance.

questions.question: Cognitive anxiety refers to:
options: Negative thoughts and mental worry about performance
questions.answer: Negative thoughts and mental worry about performance
questions.explanation: Cognitive anxiety involves mental worry, negative self-talk, and fear of failure — the psychological component of pre-competition anxiety.

questions.question: Internal imagery perspective places the athlete:
questions.answer: Internal imagery places the athlete inside their own body, imagining the movement from a first-person perspective with the sights, sounds, and sensations of performing.
questions.explanation: This contrasts with external imagery, where the athlete watches themselves as if viewing a video.

questions.question: According to Bandura, what is the strongest source of self-efficacy in sport?
options: Mastery experiences or successful performance accomplishments
questions.answer: Mastery experiences or successful performance accomplishments
questions.explanation: Successful experiences are the strongest source because they provide direct evidence of capability.

questions.question: In team sport, what does task cohesion describe?
options: Team members working together toward shared performance goals
questions.answer: Team members working together toward shared performance goals
questions.explanation: Task cohesion reflects the degree to which team members coordinate and cooperate to achieve their shared competitive objectives.

questions.question: A 'cue word' in concentration training is used to:
options: Rapidly redirect attention to a key performance element
Communicate with the coach during play
questions.answer: Rapidly redirect attention to a key performance element
questions.explanation: Cue words serve as mental triggers that quickly refocus an athlete's attention on a critical performance aspect.

questions.question: Which is the best example of an outcome goal for a high school basketball player?
options: Hold 90-degree knee bend in defensive stance
Practice ball-handling drills for 20 minutes daily
questions.answer: Win the state championship
questions.explanation: Winning the state championship is an outcome goal focused on the competitive result, partly outside the athlete's direct control.

questions.question: Broad-external attentional focus is most useful for which sport situation?
options: Scanning the field or court to read teammates, opponents, and space
Silently repeating one cue with eyes closed
questions.answer: Scanning the field or court to read teammates, opponents, and space
questions.explanation: Broad-external focus helps athletes process many outside cues at once. Students need to recognize why Scanning the field or court to read teammates, opponents, and space matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: Mental imagery activates which neural pathways?
options: Completely different pathways from physical movement
Many of the same neural pathways used during actual physical performance
Only visual processing areas of the brain
questions.answer: Many of the same neural pathways used during actual physical performance
questions.explanation: Neurological research shows mental imagery activates similar neural motor programs and brain regions as actual physical execution.

questions.question: Optimal arousal for a task requiring fine motor precision (e.g., archery) is:
questions.answer: Optimal arousal for a fine motor precision task such as archery is usually relatively low to moderate, allowing calm focus and controlled movement.
questions.explanation: Highly intense arousal can disrupt precision, while too little arousal may reduce alertness; the best level depends on the task.

questions.question: Self-Determination Theory's concept of 'competence' in sport means:
options: Feeling effective and capable in one's sport activities
questions.answer: Feeling effective and capable in one's sport activities
questions.explanation: Competence need satisfaction involves feeling capable, effective, and growing in one's athletic skills — not necessarily winning.

questions.question: Athletic burnout is characterized by which pattern?
options: Emotional and physical exhaustion, reduced accomplishment, and sport devaluation
questions.answer: Emotional and physical exhaustion, reduced accomplishment, and sport devaluation
questions.explanation: Burnout involves chronic stress and withdrawal from sport, not increased enthusiasm.

questions.question: Using imagery during injury rehabilitation helps athletes by:
options: Maintaining motor programs, reducing anxiety, and supporting psychological recovery
Only helping with upper extremity injuries
questions.answer: Maintaining motor programs, reducing anxiety, and supporting psychological recovery
questions.explanation: Imagery during rehabilitation maintains neural movement patterns, enhances efficacy, reduces fear of re-injury, and supports psychological healing.

questions.question: In Sports Psychology final concepts, WADA's primary role is to:
options: Train elite athletes in strength and conditioning
Manage and enforce anti-doping standards in international sport
questions.answer: Manage and enforce anti-doping standards in international sport
questions.explanation: WADA (World Anti-Doping Agency) develops and enforces the World Anti-Doping Code across international sports organizations.

questions.question: Early single-sport specialization before puberty is associated with:
options: Higher scholarship rates and professional contracts
Increased overuse injury and burnout risk
Better long-term athletic outcomes than multi-sport participation
questions.answer: Increased overuse injury and burnout risk
questions.explanation: Early specialization significantly increases overuse injury risk, burnout, and early dropout compared to multi-sport participation during childhood.

questions.question: The flow state is most likely to occur under which conditions?
options: Skill and challenge are balanced, with clear goals and immediate feedback
Challenge is impossible and feedback is absent
The task is unrelated to the athlete's skill
questions.answer: Skill and challenge are balanced, with clear goals and immediate feedback
questions.explanation: Flow is associated with deep focus when challenge and skill fit well. It connects the correct answer to Sports Psychology final concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: Transformational coaching in sport is characterized by:
questions.answer: Transformational coaching is characterized by inspiring athletes, modeling positive values, giving individualized support, encouraging growth, and building shared team purpose.
questions.explanation: The emphasis is on developing people and culture, not only controlling behavior or demanding performance outcomes.

questions.question: Re-injury anxiety after return to sport is:
options: A sign the athlete is not ready psychologically and physically
A normal psychological response that is common and can affect performance
Always caused by poor physical rehabilitation
Experienced by less than 1% of athletes returning from injury
questions.answer: A normal psychological response that is common and can affect performance
questions.explanation: Re-injury anxiety is a normal, common psychological response to returning from injury that may require psychological skills training to address.

questions.question: A mastery coaching climate emphasizes:
options: Personal improvement and effort over competitive outcome
Winning at every developmental age group
questions.answer: Personal improvement and effort over competitive outcome
questions.explanation: Mastery climates promote intrinsic motivation by valuing personal growth, effort, and skill development over social comparison and winning.

questions.question: Why is a pre-performance routine valuable for athletes?
options: It supports consistent focus, arousal regulation, and readiness before performance
It replaces the need for warm-up
It removes the need for practice
questions.answer: It supports consistent focus, arousal regulation, and readiness before performance
questions.explanation: A routine helps athletes prepare attention and emotion; it does not replace physical preparation.

questions.question: Sport ethics includes which principle?
options: Respecting opponents, rules, and the spirit of fair competition
Only following rules when officials are watching
questions.answer: Respecting opponents, rules, and the spirit of fair competition
questions.explanation: Sport ethics requires respect for opponents, officials, and the rules — and adherence to the spirit of fair competition beyond just letter-of-the-law compliance.

questions.question: The Kübler-Ross model is applied to sport injury because athletes:
options: Experience physical symptoms identical to grief
Often progress through emotional stages (denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance) after serious injury
Only experience two emotional stages after injury
questions.answer: Often progress through emotional stages (denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance) after serious injury
questions.explanation: Serious sport injuries trigger grief-like responses as athletes face loss of sport identity, role, and normal routine.

questions.question: Verbal persuasion as a source of self-efficacy is most effective when it comes from:
options: Credible, knowledgeable sources like coaches and experienced athletes
questions.answer: Credible, knowledgeable sources like coaches and experienced athletes
questions.explanation: Verbal encouragement is most effective when it comes from credible, knowledgeable sources whose expertise makes their assessment meaningful to the athlete.

questions.question: Which is a psychological warning sign of burnout in athletes?
questions.answer: Psychological warning signs of athlete burnout can include loss of motivation, emotional exhaustion, irritability, reduced sense of accomplishment, or dread of practice and competition.
questions.explanation: Burnout is more than normal tiredness; it includes mental and emotional withdrawal from a sport that once mattered.

questions.question: The PETTLEP imagery model stands for Physical, Environment, Task, Timing, Learning, Emotion, and:
questions.answer: Perspective
questions.explanation: PETTLEP = Physical, Environment, Task, Timing, Learning, Emotion, Perspective — a comprehensive framework for functional athletic imagery.

questions.question: A Personal Mental Performance Plan combines which key mental skills?
options: Goal setting, imagery, concentration, arousal control, and confidence strategies
questions.answer: Goal setting, imagery, concentration, arousal control, and confidence strategies
questions.explanation: A comprehensive mental performance plan integrates multiple psychological skills — goals, imagery, focus, arousal regulation, and confidence — customized for the individual.

questions.question: Fun Integration Theory research shows youth athletes define fun primarily through:
options: Trying hard, positive team dynamics, learning, and coach support
Only social activities off the field
questions.answer: Trying hard, positive team dynamics, learning, and coach support
questions.explanation: Research finds youth athletes rank effort, positive social experience, learning, and supportive coaches as their primary definitions of fun — far above winning.

questions.question: Autonomy support in sport means coaches do what?
options: Offer meaningful choices, rationale, and athlete input when appropriate
Make all decisions without athlete input
Use pressure as the only motivator
questions.answer: Offer meaningful choices, rationale, and athlete input when appropriate
questions.explanation: Autonomy-supportive coaching helps athletes feel ownership while still providing structure.

questions.question: The 'sandwich' feedback model is effective in sport coaching because:
options: It delivers corrective feedback between positive comments, increasing receptiveness
It focuses only on what athletes did wrong
questions.answer: It delivers corrective feedback between positive comments, increasing receptiveness
questions.explanation: The sandwich model wraps corrective feedback in positive framing, making athletes more receptive and less defensive to criticism.

questions.question: Which coaching practice best supports social cohesion in a team?
options: Promoting team rituals, shared goals, and opportunities for athletes to bond
Separating the best athletes from lower performers
questions.answer: Promoting team rituals, shared goals, and opportunities for athletes to bond
questions.explanation: Social cohesion is built through shared experiences, team rituals, mutual support, and deliberate relationship-building opportunities.

questions.question: Mindfulness training benefits athletes primarily by:
questions.answer: Mindfulness training benefits athletes by helping them stay present, notice thoughts and emotions without overreacting, manage pressure, and refocus after mistakes.
questions.explanation: The performance value comes from attention control and emotional regulation during practice and competition.
